sections.paragraph: a lion, so called from his roaring, see לָבָא , a word altogether poetic, Gen 49:9 Num 24:9 Deu 33:20 Job 4:11 38:39 Isa 5:29 30:6 , etc. (Arab. لَبْأَةُ, لَبِيءَةُ, لُبْوءَةُ, also لَبْوَةُ a lioness; Copt. ⲗⲁⲃⲟⲓ a bear [also a lion and lioness].) Bechart considers, Hieroz. i. p. 719, that this word does not signify a lion, but a lioness, principally influenced by the passage, Eze 19:2, and by an etymology, from لبا to draw the first milk, IV. to suckle with the first milk; but in Eze. loc. cit., there occurs the form לְבִיָּא, and the proposed etymology lacks even the appearance of truth. [In Thes., however, Bochart’s supposition is treated with more favour, although on different grounds, especially as being more suited to the context of the passages.]
